museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: March is live-service testing and Switch 2 Pokemon.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: March 2026 was a month where gaming felt both overstuffed and unsettled. The release calendar came in heavy, with Marathon, Pokémon Pokopia, Slay the Spire 2, Crimson Desert, Death Stranding 2 on PC, and a pile-up of remasters giving players far too much to keep up with. GDC’s newly expanded Festival of Gaming put the industry’s future-facing obsessions on display, from AI and RTX graphics tech to publishing, tools, and platform strategy. Nintendo also gave the month a talking point with Pokémon Pokopia’s Game-Key Card release, a very modern reminder that even “physical” games were becoming download-shaped. Then, right at the end of the month, the Ubisoft lawsuit over The Crew’s shutdown sharpened the bigger ownership debate. March was not just busy; it felt like a snapshot of where games were heading next.

items.title: Marathon launches on March 5.
items.body: The long-awaited Bungle shooter Marathon arrives. The biggest problem it faces is that ARC Raiders got there first and swept up all the players. A poorly-received playtest also did not help.

items.title: Pokemon Pokopia also lands on March 5.
items.body: When the world needed an even cosier version of Pokémon, up popped Pokopia (that is a solid tongue-twister). It annoyed a lot of Switch fans as it was Switch 2 only for no obvious reason other than to sell consoles.

items.title: Slay the Spire 2 begins early access on March 5.
items.body: Perhaps the greatest Deckbuilder to date finally got a sequel, and man was it good. Steam buckled, millions were made, and somewhere the game drove everybody mad. What a follow-up.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; recent-history claims should be rechecked during human edit.

items.title: Monster Hunter Stories 3 arrives on March 13.
items.body: The third in the series of Monster Hunter RPG spin-offs arrived and subsequently was well received by fans of the franchise.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; recent-history claims should be rechecked during human edit.

items.title: Crimson Desert lands on March 19.
items.body: Crimson Desert arrived from Pearl Abyss and immediately sold three million copies making it a mega-March for the publisher.

items.title: World of Warcraft: Midnight
items.body: World of Warcraft: Midnight is the highly celebrated eleventh expansion for the MMORPG, and the second chapter of the Worldsoul Saga.

items.title: Marathon
items.body: Explore the lost colony of Tau Ceti IV, scavenging valuable loot in a high-stakes survival first person shooter from the creators of Halo and Destiny.

items.title: Pokemon Pokopia
items.body: Pokémon Pokopia is a social simulation game co-developed by Game Freak and Koei Tecmo's Omega Force and published by Nintendo and The Pokémon Company for the Nintendo Switch 2. It is a spin-off of the main Pokémon series.

items.title: Slay the Spire 2
items.body: The iconic roguelike deckbuilder returns. Craft a unique deck, encounter bizarre creatures, and discover relics of immense power in Slay the Spire 2!

items.title: Monster Hunter Stories 3: Twisted Reflection
items.body: The third entry in the Monster Hunter Stories RPG series is here! Twin Rathalos, born in a twist of fate. Monster Hunter Stories is an RPG series set in the Monster Hunter world, where you can become a Rider, raising and bonding with your favorite monsters.
items.platform: PS5, Xbox Series X/S, Switch 2, PC

items.title: WWE 2K26
items.body: The show never stops in WWE 2K26! Step into CM Punk’s 2K Showcase, dominate with 400+ Superstars and Legends, and unleash chaos with all-new match types.
items.platform: PS5, Xbox Series X/S, Switch 2, PC

items.title: Crimson Desert
items.body: Crimson Desert is an open-world action-adventure set on the continent of Pywel. Join Kliff on his journey to rebuild the Greymane faction and to save the land from a looming threat. From vast wilderness and cities to ruins and the mysterious Abyss, forge your path through battles and discovery.

items.title: Screamer
items.body: High-octane action and anime aesthetics collide in this arcade racing game, featuring fighting mechanics and a storyline that hits hard. In this world, some race for glory while others seek power or revenge. Every race is a fight and every battle is personal.

items.title: Super Meat Boy 3D
items.body: The infamous, tough-as-nails platformer – now in 3D!

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Platform, subscription and buying context around the month.

items.title: ROG Xbox Ally X starts clipping your best moments
items.body: Xbox Insiders were invited to test Highlight Reels on the ROG Xbox Ally X, with automatic clips created after supported games. It made Microsoft’s handheld push feel less like “Windows on a small screen” and more like a proper Xbox-flavoured device.

items.title: Nvidia uses GDC to push DLSS 4.5 and path tracing harder
items.body: Nvidia’s GDC showing was all about making high-end PC gaming look smarter, faster, and more expensive. DLSS 4.5, multi-frame generation, RTX Remix effects, and path-traced showcases kept the graphics-card arms race right at the centre of the industry conversation.

items.title: Project Helix points to the next Xbox
items.body: Microsoft talked up Project Helix as its next-generation Xbox console, built with AMD and designed to bring console and PC gaming closer together. It was less a normal console tease and more a sign that Xbox hardware was becoming a bridge between the living room and Windows.
items.sourceNote: Gematsu and PC Gamer Forza reporting.

items.title: Switch 2 makes old Switch games look better in handheld mode
items.body: Nintendo’s Handheld Mode Boost gave older Switch games a cleaner life on Switch 2 by letting many run closer to their docked profile while still being played portably. It was a small update, but a big quality-of-life win for anyone dragging a huge old library onto new hardware.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How players discovered, argued about and shared the month.

items.title: Single-player games started borrowing other people’s choices
items.body: Tides of Tomorrow pushed its “Story-Link” idea: your solo adventure could be shaped by decisions made by previous players. Nice little card because it captures how even single-player games were becoming quietly networked.

items.title: Roblox tried to make chat feel less like a wall of hashtags
items.body: Roblox outlined changes to make chat more natural while still keeping safety controls in place. It’s a very 2026 problem: players wanted normal conversation, platforms wanted fewer disasters, and everyone was stuck negotiating with filters.

items.title: PlayStation’s prestige games kept growing online appendages
items.body: Ghost of Yōtei Legends showed how the old “big single-player PlayStation game” model now often came with a separate online co-op layer, complete with PS Plus requirements for multiplayer.

items.title: The Crew shutdown became a courtroom argument
items.body: French consumer group UFC-Que Choisir sued Ubisoft over The Crew being made unplayable after its servers closed, with the case tied to the wider Stop Killing Games movement. This is probably the strongest March card: online games stopped being just a customer-service complaint and became a legal fight over what buying a game actually means.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading from the player side of recent history.

items.title: The calendar got noisy fast
items.body: March felt like one of those months where every kind of player suddenly had homework: shooters, RPGs, cosy games, sports sims, remasters, early access darlings, and big open-world adventures all shouting at once.

items.title: The backlog became the main boss
items.body: There was no single mood to settle into. One minute you were looking at a huge fantasy adventure, the next at a stylish indie, a returning cult name, or a game you had already half-promised yourself you would definitely get around to this time.

items.title: Old names kept barging back in
items.body: March had that very 2026 feeling of the past refusing to stay archived. Remasters, remakes, sequels, and familiar brands made the month feel less like a clean slate and more like gaming history being dragged back onto the front page.

items.title: Everything felt slightly too big
items.body: Even the exciting stuff carried a sense of scale creep. Bigger worlds, longer campaigns, wider launch plans, more platforms, more modes, more updates. March was fun, but it also felt like gaming had forgotten how to travel light.
